Minimal HTML skeleton with single root div and external bundled JavaScript assets typical of React SPA phishing pages

sourcePatterns.description: Context menu disabled via addEventListener and preventDefault — common anti-analysis technique in phishing kits
sourcePatterns.note: Listed in rule writing guide as NOT in ruleset. Moderate weight because some legitimate image-protection sites do this, but very common in phishkits to prevent source inspection.

sourcePatterns.description: Right-click disabled via inline oncontextmenu returning false
sourcePatterns.note: Listed in rule writing guide as NOT in ruleset. Low weight — appears on some image-protection sites too, but stacks with other anti-analysis signals on phishing pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Keyboard shortcut interception blocking Ctrl+U (view source) — phishkit anti-forensics
sourcePatterns.note: Ctrl+U blocking is highly specific to phishing pages — legitimate sites have no reason to prevent viewing source code. Separate from DevTools/F12 blocking already in ruleset.

sourcePatterns.description: Keyboard shortcut interception blocking Ctrl+S (save page) — phishkit anti-forensics
sourcePatterns.note: Blocking page save combined with ctrlKey check is phishkit-specific anti-forensics. Some web apps override Ctrl+S for saving data, but the combination with preventDefault in the keyboard handler is a strong indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Keyboard shortcut interception blocking Ctrl+Shift+I/J/C (DevTools variations) — phishkit anti-analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Blocks DevTools opening via Ctrl+Shift+I, Ctrl+Shift+J, or Ctrl+Shift+C. Distinct from the existing devtools-block rule which covers F12/keyCode 123. These alternative shortcut blocks are characteristic of Whisper 2FA and similar advanced phishing kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Infinite debugger statement loop used to crash browser DevTools — anti-analysis watchdog technique
sourcePatterns.note: The setInterval + debugger combo creates a watchdog loop that freezes DevTools when opened. No legitimate website uses setInterval to repeatedly trigger debugger statements. Highly specific to phishing kits like Whisper 2FA.

sourcePatterns.description: Function constructor used to generate debugger traps dynamically — advanced anti-debugging technique
sourcePatterns.note: Using Function constructor to create debugger traps is a hallmark of obfuscated phishing kits. Legitimate code never dynamically constructs debugger statements via Function(). 25 literal characters provides strong specificity.

sourcePatterns.description: Checks navigator.webdriver property to detect headless browsers and automated scanners
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing kits check navigator.webdriver to detect headless Chrome and automated security scanners. Some legitimate bot protection does this, but combined with other signals it is a strong phishing indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Console object manipulation to detect DevTools open state — phishkit anti-analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Overriding console properties via defineProperty is used by phishkits like Whisper 2FA to detect when DevTools console is opened. If detected, the page wipes its content. Legitimate sites don't redefine console properties.

sourcePatterns.description: User selection disabled across entire page body or html element — prevents text copy for analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling text selection on the entire page body prevents analysts from easily copying text for investigation. Low weight because some legitimate sites do this, but it stacks with other anti-analysis signals.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS disabling both text selection and copy across page — anti-forensics technique
sourcePatterns.note: Combining user-select:none with webkit-user-drag:none is overkill for legitimate purposes. This combination prevents any form of content copying or dragging, which is used by phishing kits to prevent analysis.

sourcePatterns.description: Timing-based bot detection measuring interaction speed — phishkit anti-automation
sourcePatterns.note: Measuring time between page load and user interaction to detect bots is a phishing kit anti-automation technique. Pages require minimum human-speed interaction before revealing the phishing content.

sourcePatterns.description: Mouse movement tracking required before revealing phishing content
sourcePatterns.note: Requiring mouse movement before revealing login forms is an anti-bot technique. Automated scanners that just load the page won't see the phishing content. The mousemove-to-reveal-form chain is specific to phishkits.

sourcePatterns.description: Page content wiped or replaced when DevTools detection fires — anti-forensics technique
sourcePatterns.note: Wiping page content (body.innerHTML = '', document.write('')) when DevTools or debugger is detected is a phishkit self-destruct mechanism. Legitimate sites never wipe themselves based on debugging state.

sourcePatterns.description: HTTP referer/referrer validation that blocks or redirects direct visits — scanner evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for an empty referrer and redirecting away is a scanner evasion technique. Security scanners navigate directly to the URL without a referrer, so the phishing page redirects them to a benign site.

sourcePatterns.description: User agent filtering to show phishing content only to mobile browsers
sourcePatterns.note: Filtering by mobile user agent is a phishing technique targeting mobile users specifically. SMS phishing (smishing) campaigns show phishing only to mobile browsers and redirect desktop/scanner visits to benign sites.

sourcePatterns.description: Geolocation API used to gate phishing content display by country
sourcePatterns.note: Using the Geolocation API to determine the victim's country and conditionally display phishing content is a targeted campaign technique. Pages show benign content to non-target countries and phishing to targets.

sourcePatterns.description: Screen resolution and color depth fingerprinting combined — bot detection via display properties
sourcePatterns.note: Collecting screen resolution and color depth together is a browser fingerprinting technique used by phishing kits to detect headless browsers (which often report unusual display properties). Low weight because analytics scripts do this too.

sourcePatterns.description: Empty referrer check redirecting to legitimate site — evasion of direct URL visits from security scanners
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting away when document.referrer is empty blocks direct URL visits (how security scanners access pages). The phishing page only works when accessed through the original phishing link.

sourcePatterns.description: Hidden form field named botcheck or honeypot — bot detection mechanism in phishing kit
sourcePatterns.note: Hidden fields specifically named 'botcheck' or 'honeypot' are phishing kit bot detection mechanisms. If a bot fills these invisible fields, the kit discards the submission. Legitimate sites use standard CAPTCHA services.

sourcePatterns.description: Script element removes itself from DOM after execution — DOM vanishing technique used by Tycoon 2FA
sourcePatterns.note: The DOM Vanishing Act technique has scripts remove themselves from the DOM after execution, leaving no trace for security tools that inspect the page's source post-render. This is a signature Tycoon 2FA technique.

sourcePatterns.description: Detection of Selenium, WebDriver, or Puppeteer automation frameworks
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing kits check for Selenium/WebDriver/Puppeteer/PhantomJS/Nightmare automation framework globals to detect security scanners. If detected, the page redirects to a benign site. Multiple framework checks in one page is highly specific to anti-analysis code.

sourcePatterns.description: Detection of Burp Suite penetration testing proxy — anti-analyst evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for Burp Suite strings is a definitive anti-analyst behavior. No legitimate website checks for the presence of security testing tools. This is used by Tycoon 2FA and Whisper 2FA to detect researcher environments.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect to about:blank when security tool or sandbox is detected — Tycoon 2FA evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting to about:blank when a security tool is detected is a Tycoon 2FA signature behavior. When the kit detects debugger, Selenium, or Burp Suite, it sends the browser to a blank page to prevent analysis.

sourcePatterns.description: Timing-based debugger detection — measures execution delay to detect breakpoints
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA measures time before and after a debugger statement. If the debugger pauses execution for more than 0.1 seconds, it assumes a researcher is present and redirects to google.com. This timing+debugger+redirect chain is definitive anti-analysis.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect to google.com or other major site when bot/sandbox check fails — phishing kit bailout
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting to Google, Bing, Rakuten, or Tesla when anti-bot checks fail is a common phishing kit bailout. Tycoon 2FA redirects to google.com on debugger detection; other kits use Rakuten or Tesla. The redirect to a benign popular site hides the phishing from analysts.

sourcePatterns.description: Copy event listener intercepting clipboard operations — prevents copying page content for analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Intercepting the copy event prevents analysts from copying page content for investigation. Phishing kits block copy operations to prevent URL, text, or source code from being easily shared with security teams.

sourcePatterns.description: Paste event listener intercepting paste operations — prevents password manager paste
sourcePatterns.note: Blocking paste operations prevents password managers from filling in credentials. Phishing kits do this because password managers often detect that the current domain doesn't match the saved credential's domain.

sourcePatterns.description: Text selection event blocked via selectstart listener — prevents content analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Blocking text selection via the selectstart event prevents analysts from selecting and copying text content. Combined with copy blocking and right-click disabling, this creates a full anti-analysis lock on page content.

sourcePatterns.description: Page content hidden or wiped when tab loses focus — anti-tab-switching analysis technique
sourcePatterns.note: Detecting tab visibility changes and hiding/wiping content when the user switches tabs prevents screenshot tools and analysis in other tabs from capturing the phishing page. This is advanced anti-forensics.

sourcePatterns.description: Print stylesheet hides page content — prevents printing phishing page for evidence
sourcePatterns.note: Print-specific CSS that hides all content prevents users and analysts from printing the phishing page as evidence. Legitimate sites don't hide their entire content when printing.

sourcePatterns.description: Drag and drop events disabled across page — prevents content extraction via dragging
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling drag/drop prevents analysts from dragging page content into other applications for analysis. This is a supporting anti-analysis signal that stacks with copy/paste/select blocking.

sourcePatterns.description: Page redirects or hides when window loses focus — anti-analysis tab-switching detection
sourcePatterns.note: Detecting window blur (losing focus) and redirecting or wiping content is an anti-analysis technique. When an analyst switches to another window to take notes or use tools, the phishing page destroys itself.

sourcePatterns.description: Console repeatedly cleared to hide debugging output — anti-analysis technique
sourcePatterns.note: Repeatedly clearing the console via setInterval prevents analysts from seeing debug output, network errors, or console warnings that might reveal the phishing kit's behavior.

sourcePatterns.description: MutationObserver watching for DOM changes by security tools — anti-tampering defense
sourcePatterns.note: Using MutationObserver to detect DOM modifications and self-destruct or redirect is an anti-tampering technique. If a security tool injects elements into the page, the observer detects it and triggers evasion.

sourcePatterns.description: Image error handler used to detect DevTools open state — covert debugging detection
sourcePatterns.note: Creating an Image object and overriding its id/toString property with Object.defineProperty to detect DevTools is a known Whisper 2FA technique. When DevTools tries to display the object, the getter fires and detects the open console.

sourcePatterns.description: Window size comparison used to detect DevTools panel — outerWidth vs innerWidth check
sourcePatterns.note: Comparing outerWidth/outerHeight to innerWidth/innerHeight detects whether DevTools is docked to the side or bottom of the browser window (the difference exceeds a threshold). This is a stealthier alternative to debugger-based detection.

sourcePatterns.description: Performance API entries checked for debugging or sandboxed environment markers
sourcePatterns.note: Analyzing performance entries to detect sandboxed or instrumented environments is an advanced anti-analysis technique. Automated tools often leave distinctive performance entry patterns.

sourcePatterns.description: WebRTC used to discover victim's real IP address — sandbox/VPN detection fingerprint
sourcePatterns.note: Using WebRTC to discover the victim's real IP address (bypassing VPN/proxy) is a fingerprinting technique used by phishing kits to detect security researchers who browse via VPN or corporate proxy.

sourcePatterns.description: Global error handler suppresses all JavaScript errors — hides phishing kit debug output
sourcePatterns.note: Setting window.onerror to always return true suppresses all JavaScript errors from appearing in the console. Phishing kits use this to prevent error messages from revealing the kit's internal operation.

sourcePatterns.description: Email domain validation filtering out personal email providers — enterprise-targeted phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Rejecting free email providers (Gmail, Yahoo, Hotmail, AOL) and only accepting corporate email addresses is a Sneaky 2FA technique that specifically targets enterprise users. This filtering is a definitive phishing signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Page CSS designed exclusively for small/mobile screens — smishing page targeting mobile
sourcePatterns.note: Pages styled exclusively for mobile screen widths (400-599px) with login/payment forms are SMS phishing pages. They're designed to look good only on phones because victims arrive via text message links.

sourcePatterns.description: Viewport meta tag with user-scalable=no and fixed width — mobile phishing page lockdown
sourcePatterns.note: Viewport locked to device-width with scaling disabled prevents users from zooming out to see the full page URL or inspect the page. Supporting signal for mobile-targeting phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Email address validated against external API before showing phishing content — targeted phishing gate
sourcePatterns.note: Validating the victim's email via API before showing phishing content ensures only real corporate email users see the phishing page. Sneaky 2FA and similar enterprise-targeted kits use this to filter out security researchers.

sourcePatterns.description: Email domain checked against corporate domain list before proceeding — enterprise targeting filter
sourcePatterns.note: Checking the email domain and rejecting free providers (Gmail, Yahoo, Hotmail) is the Sneaky 2FA enterprise targeting filter. The phishing page only works for corporate email addresses.

sourcePatterns.description: WordPress login cookie checked to avoid showing malware to site admins
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for WordPress admin cookies and hiding malicious content from logged-in site admins is a SocGholish/ClearFake evasion technique. The site appears normal to the admin but shows fake updates to visitors.

sourcePatterns.description: Browser history flooded to prevent back button from leaving page — page lock technique
sourcePatterns.note: Flooding browser history with pushState/replaceState entries in a loop prevents the back button from working. Victims can't navigate away from tech support scam or phishing pages using the back button.

sourcePatterns.description: Beforeunload event preventing page exit with confirmation dialog — page retention technique
sourcePatterns.note: Setting a beforeunload handler with returnValue or preventDefault shows a 'Are you sure you want to leave?' dialog. Tech support scams and phishing pages use this to prevent victims from closing the tab.

sourcePatterns.description: Canvas fingerprinting — drawing hidden shapes to generate unique browser fingerprint
sourcePatterns.note: Canvas fingerprinting draws hidden text/shapes and converts to a data URL to create a unique browser fingerprint. Phishing kits use this to track victims and detect sandbox environments with non-standard renderers.

sourcePatterns.description: WebGL renderer and vendor queried for browser fingerprinting — sandbox detection
sourcePatterns.note: Querying WebGL renderer and vendor strings detects virtual machines and headless browsers that report generic GPU names like 'SwiftShader' or 'llvmpipe'. Phishing kits use this to filter out security scanners.

sourcePatterns.description: AudioContext fingerprinting — oscillator output analyzed for unique browser signature
sourcePatterns.note: AudioContext fingerprinting creates an oscillator and analyzes its output to generate a unique browser fingerprint. Each browser/OS combination produces slightly different audio processing results.

sourcePatterns.description: Timezone and locale checked to target specific geographic regions — targeted phishing gate
sourcePatterns.note: Checking timezone and browser language together to gate content by geography is a targeted phishing technique. Pages show phishing content only to victims in the target country and redirect others.

sourcePatterns.description: Network connection type checked to verify mobile device — smishing page verification
sourcePatterns.note: Checking navigator.connection for cellular network types verifies the visitor is on a mobile device. Smishing pages use this to ensure only mobile users (who arrived via SMS link) see the phishing content.

sourcePatterns.description: Touch event support checked to verify mobile device — smishing page filter
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for touch event support to verify the visitor is on a mobile/tablet device. SMS phishing pages that should only be viewed on phones use this to redirect desktop visitors.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect to google.com/404 when bot or invalid access detected — Mamba 2FA antibot
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting to google.com/404 on invalid access or bot detection is a specific Mamba 2FA antibot signature. The kit redirects to Google's 404 page rather than google.com homepage.

sourcePatterns.description: localStorage and sessionStorage cleared on page unload — anti-forensics evidence destruction
sourcePatterns.note: Clearing all storage when the page unloads destroys forensic evidence of the phishing interaction. Credentials staged in storage during multi-step flows are wiped when the victim navigates away.

sourcePatterns.description: Service worker caches deleted to prevent cached content analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Deleting service worker caches prevents forensic analysis of cached phishing page content. After the attack, cached evidence is destroyed so investigators can't reconstruct the phishing page.

sourcePatterns.description: IntersectionObserver delays phishing form injection until element is visible — lazy-load evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Using IntersectionObserver to inject the phishing form only when the user scrolls to that part of the page delays form insertion until actual user interaction. Security scanners that don't scroll won't trigger the form injection.

sourcePatterns.description: IdleDetector API used to trigger phishing after user returns — attention-based triggering
sourcePatterns.note: Using the IdleDetector API to wait until the user returns from being idle before showing phishing content ensures the victim is actively looking at the screen. This maximizes the chance of credential entry.

sourcePatterns.description: Desktop user agents redirected to different content than mobile — mobile-only phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for desktop user agents and redirecting or hiding content ensures only mobile users see the phishing page. Security researchers primarily use desktop browsers, so this filters them out.

sourcePatterns.description: Screen width checked to verify mobile device — shows phishing only on small screens
sourcePatterns.note: Checking screen width to only show phishing content on mobile-sized screens (< 768px) is a smishing page technique. The page shows a blank or benign page on desktop to evade scanning.

sourcePatterns.description: Device orientation/motion events checked for mobile bot detection — accelerometer verification
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for device orientation/motion sensor data verifies the page is running on a real mobile device with an accelerometer. Emulators and desktop browsers don't produce real sensor data.

sourcePatterns.description: Pull-to-refresh gesture disabled via overscroll-behavior — prevents mobile page reload
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling pull-to-refresh via overscroll-behavior on a login page prevents mobile users from accidentally refreshing the page, which could reset the phishing state. Also prevents the user from seeing the URL flash during refresh.

sourcePatterns.description: Virtual keyboard presence detected via viewport resize — verifies real mobile interaction
sourcePatterns.note: Detecting the virtual keyboard opening by monitoring viewport height changes (the viewport shrinks when the keyboard appears) verifies the user is on a real mobile device. Emulators don't consistently replicate this behavior.

sourcePatterns.description: Amazon brand impersonation detected through specific CSS classes and DOM structure
sourcePatterns.note: Combination of Amazon-specific CSS classes and JavaScript files that only appear on Amazon impersonation sites

sourcePatterns.note: Detects impersonation through brand-named subdomains on suspicious TLDs - legitimate brands use their own domains

sourcePatterns.description: Adobe PDF document verification page requesting login credentials to access protected files
sourcePatterns.note: Combines Adobe PDF branding with file verification pretext and password collection - legitimate Adobe services don't require arbitrary logins for PDF access

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft sign-in page UI elements and terminology impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: Combines Microsoft's distinctive 'Sign in to your account' header with secondary UI elements or domain references

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft MFA authentication flow text — 'Approve sign in request' and organization sign-in page language used in Microsoft login impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: These exact phrases are from Microsoft's MFA/authenticator flow. Legitimate occurrences are only on login.microsoftonline.com and login.microsoft.com. Finding both strings together on any other domain is a strong Microsoft impersonation signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft-specific 'Other ways to sign in' text combined with password field — Microsoft login page impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: The phrase 'Other ways to sign in' is specific to Microsoft's login UX. Combined with a password field on a non-Microsoft domain, this is a strong impersonation signal. Google uses 'More ways to sign in' and other providers use different wording.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft-specific 'Stay signed in?' post-authentication prompt found alongside sign-in elements
sourcePatterns.note: The 'Stay signed in?' prompt with checkbox is specific to Microsoft's KMSI (Keep Me Signed In) flow. Finding this alongside other login elements on a non-Microsoft domain indicates a full authentication flow clone. Weight kept moderate as some SSO integrations may embed this legitimately.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft-specific sign-in flow text phrases appearing together, indicating brand impersonation
sourcePatterns.patternString: Sign in with a security key[\s\S]{0,3000}Sign-in options|Sign-in options[\s\S]{0,3000}Sign in with a security key
sourcePatterns.note: The phrase 'Sign in with a security key' combined with 'Sign-in options' is specific to Microsoft's login flow. Legitimate Microsoft domains are excluded by the domain allowlist. Non-Microsoft domains showing this exact combination are impersonating the Microsoft login experience.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft login page impersonation with 'No account? Create one!' and sign-in elements
sourcePatterns.patternString: No account\?\s*Create one![\s\S]{0,5000}(?:Sign-in options|sign in with a security key)
sourcePatterns.note: The exact phrasing 'No account? Create one!' is Microsoft-specific copy used on their login page. Combined with other Microsoft sign-in elements on a non-Microsoft domain, this is strong evidence of brand impersonation. This text combination does not appear on other major login providers.

sourcePatterns.description: Clearbit Logo API used to dynamically fetch victim's company logo for targeted phishing
sourcePatterns.note: The Clearbit Logo API lets phishing kits dynamically load any company's logo based on the victim's email domain, enabling mass personalization. Legitimate login pages use their own static assets, not third-party logo APIs.

sourcePatterns.description: Azure AD CDN used to dynamically load branding based on victim email domain — personalized phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA and other Microsoft-targeting kits dynamically load logos and backgrounds from Microsoft's AADCDN based on the victim's email domain. This makes the phishing page match the victim's organization's real login branding. The AADCDN + branding/logo combination on a non-Microsoft domain is highly suspicious.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS custom properties set to exact brand colors of major companies — pixel-perfect impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: CSS custom properties using exact hex values for Microsoft (#0078d4), Facebook (#1877f2), Google (#ea4335), Amazon (#ff9900), etc. indicate brand color impersonation. Low weight because legitimate brand-themed sites exist, but stacks as a supporting signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Major brand logo embedded as inline SVG to avoid external image loading detection
sourcePatterns.note: Embedding brand logos as inline SVG avoids making external requests that could be tracked or blocked. Phishing kits inline SVG brand logos so the page looks legitimate without loading any external assets.

sourcePatterns.description: Theme-color meta tag set to match brand colors — mobile browser chrome impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: Setting the theme-color meta tag to exact brand colors (Microsoft blue, Facebook blue, Google red, Amazon orange, Spotify green, Netflix red) makes the mobile browser's address bar and status bar match the impersonated brand.
