sourcePatterns.description: Slack incoming webhook URL used for credential exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Slack webhook URLs with workspace and bot token components in page JavaScript are definitive exfiltration signals. No legitimate login page sends data to Slack webhooks. 40+ literal characters.

sourcePatterns.description: EmailJS SDK used to exfiltrate credentials via email directly from browser
sourcePatterns.note: EmailJS is a legitimate service, but using it to send data that includes password field values is a definitive phishkit exfiltration pattern. Legitimate sites never email passwords from client-side JavaScript.

sourcePatterns.description: Form action set to a data: URI — obfuscated credential submission endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Using a data: URI as a form action is never legitimate. This is a phishkit technique to either execute inline JavaScript or encode the submission handler, evading URL-based detection. Definitive phishing signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Keystroke accumulation via keypress/keydown handler — credential keylogger pattern
sourcePatterns.note: Keypress event handlers that accumulate keystrokes into a string or array are credential keyloggers. They capture everything typed including passwords without requiring form submission. Definitive phishing signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Window.opener manipulation combined with about:blank — tabnabbing phishing technique
sourcePatterns.note: Manipulating window.opener.location or creating about:blank windows is the tabnabbing technique where a background tab is redirected to a phishing page while the user is on another tab. Legitimate sites don't manipulate opener windows.

sourcePatterns.description: History.replaceState used to mask the URL bar with a legitimate-looking URL
sourcePatterns.note: Using history.replaceState to make the URL bar show a legitimate brand domain is a phishkit URL masking technique. The actual URL is the phishing domain, but the user sees a familiar brand URL.

sourcePatterns.description: Credentials exfiltrated via image beacon (new Image().src with credential data in URL)
sourcePatterns.note: Creating an Image object and setting its src to a URL containing credential data is a classic phishkit exfiltration technique that bypasses CORS restrictions. No legitimate site sends passwords via image requests.

sourcePatterns.description: ClickFix technique — clipboard content set via navigator.clipboard.writeText with command payload
sourcePatterns.note: The ClickFix technique writes a malicious command to the clipboard via navigator.clipboard.writeText, then instructs the user to paste it into Run dialog. This is the #1 initial access technique of 2025. Definitive malware signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Document.execCommand used to silently copy content to clipboard — pastejacking attack
sourcePatterns.note: Using execCommand('copy') followed by references to command execution (PowerShell, cmd, Run dialog instructions) is the older variant of ClickFix pastejacking. The clipboard is silently replaced with a malicious command.

sourcePatterns.description: PHP endpoint returning a result keyword parsed by JavaScript — phishkit C2 communication
sourcePatterns.note: A PHP endpoint returning status keywords like 'success', 'valid', 'next', or 'step2' that are parsed in JavaScript indicates a phishkit communicating with its backend to advance the credential capture flow.

sourcePatterns.description: All link clicks intercepted and reported to external endpoint — phishing activity tracking
sourcePatterns.note: Intercepting all link clicks and reporting them to an external endpoint is phishkit telemetry — tracking victim behavior to optimize the campaign. Legitimate analytics use dedicated libraries, not raw click interception on all anchors.

sourcePatterns.description: DNS prefetch hint pointing to suspicious endpoint — phishing kit preconnects to exfil infrastructure
sourcePatterns.note: DNS prefetch hints to suspicious TLDs or PHP/ASP endpoints indicate the phishing page is preconnecting to its exfiltration infrastructure for faster credential stealing.

sourcePatterns.description: Phone number extracted and sent via Telegram or HTTP — voice phishing data harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Extracting a phone number field value and sending it to an external endpoint is phishkit data harvesting for voice phishing (vishing) follow-up attacks.

sourcePatterns.description: Clipboard content silently overwritten with attacker-controlled data — credential/command injection
sourcePatterns.note: Silently writing decoded/deobfuscated content to the clipboard via navigator.clipboard.writeText is used in ClickFix and credential-swapping attacks. The combination of clipboard write with decoding functions indicates the written content is intentionally hidden.

sourcePatterns.description: Service worker registration on phishing page — persistence and offline caching technique
sourcePatterns.note: Registering a service worker on a phishing page enables caching of the phishing content for offline access and persistence. This is an advanced technique that keeps the phishing page available even if the domain is taken down.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript file loaded with cache-busting hash in filename — BlackForce kit signature
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript files with cache-busting hashes in filenames like index-a1b2c3d4.js are characteristic of BlackForce phishing kit. The hashes force browsers to load fresh copies, bypassing cached security analysis.

sourcePatterns.description: FormSubmit.co service used as credential exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: FormSubmit.co is a form-to-email service heavily abused by phishing kits for credential exfiltration. The 24+ character hex ID in the URL identifies the attacker's submission endpoint. Legitimate sites rarely use FormSubmit.co for login forms.

sourcePatterns.description: Getform.io service used as credential exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Getform.io is another form backend service abused by phishing kits. The long hex ID in the URL is the attacker's endpoint. Like FormSubmit.co, this is a fire-and-forget credential exfiltration method.

sourcePatterns.description: Web3Forms service used as credential exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Web3Forms is a form submission API abused for credential exfiltration. The api.web3forms.com/submit endpoint receives form data without requiring server-side code, making it popular with phishing kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Fabform.io service used as credential exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Fabform.io is a form backend service that is abused by phishing kits as an exfiltration endpoint. No server-side code needed — the kit submits credentials directly to the service.

sourcePatterns.description: Beacon API used to track page load events to attacker endpoint — phishing campaign telemetry
sourcePatterns.note: Using sendBeacon to POST JSON data to an external endpoint is a phishing kit telemetry pattern. The kit tracks page loads, user interactions, and campaign metrics via fire-and-forget beacon calls.

sourcePatterns.description: Window.name used as covert data channel between pages — phishing redirect data passing
sourcePatterns.note: Setting window.name with encoded credential data is a covert data-passing technique. window.name persists across page navigations, so phishing kits use it to transfer stolen data between domains without URL parameters.

sourcePatterns.description: XMLHttpRequest polling at regular intervals — phishing kit command-and-control communication
sourcePatterns.note: Periodic XHR polling via setInterval is used by phishing kits that communicate with a C2 server to receive instructions (e.g., 'show MFA prompt now' or 'redirect to brand site'). This is characteristic of real-time AiTM phishing flows.

sourcePatterns.description: Fetch API polling at regular intervals — phishing kit C2 communication pattern
sourcePatterns.note: Regular fetch polling to an external URL is the modern equivalent of XHR polling for C2 communication. AiTM phishing kits poll the attacker server to synchronize the multi-step authentication flow.

sourcePatterns.description: QR code dynamically rendered on HTML5 canvas — phishing page with embedded QR redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Dynamically rendering QR codes on canvas that contain redirect URLs is a quishing (QR phishing) technique. The QR code pushes victims from desktop to mobile where security tools are weaker.

sourcePatterns.description: QR code generation library loaded on a page with login elements — quishing indicator
sourcePatterns.note: Loading a QR code generation library on a page that also has login elements indicates a quishing attack. The page either generates a QR code for mobile redirect or uses it as a social engineering lure.

sourcePatterns.description: HTML smuggling — Blob URL created for automatic file download from inline data
sourcePatterns.note: Creating a Blob from inline data and triggering an automatic download by clicking an invisible anchor is the HTML smuggling technique. The malicious file is constructed entirely in the browser from encoded data, bypassing network-level file scanning.

sourcePatterns.description: MHTML content constructed in JavaScript for HTML smuggling delivery
sourcePatterns.note: MHTML content headers constructed in JavaScript indicate HTML smuggling that assembles an MHTML file in-browser for download. MHTML files can contain active content that executes on the victim's machine.

sourcePatterns.description: UseBasin.com form backend used as credential exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: UseBasin.com is a form backend service abused for credential exfiltration. The hex ID in the URL identifies the attacker's endpoint.

sourcePatterns.description: Formspark.io form backend used as credential exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Formspark is a form submission service abused by phishing kits. Both submit-form.com and formspark.io domains are used.

sourcePatterns.description: SheetDB API used to exfiltrate credentials to Google Sheets
sourcePatterns.note: SheetDB turns Google Sheets into an API. Phishing kits use it to write stolen credentials directly to the attacker's Google Sheet without any server-side code.

sourcePatterns.description: Google Apps Script used as credential exfiltration endpoint — serverless exfil
sourcePatterns.note: Google Apps Script endpoints (/macros/s/.../exec) are heavily abused as serverless credential exfiltration handlers. The script writes captured data to Google Sheets. The long deployment ID is specific to each attacker.

sourcePatterns.description: Pipedream webhook used as credential exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Pipedream.net webhooks are automation endpoints abused for credential exfiltration. The service provides free webhook endpoints that can forward data to any destination.

sourcePatterns.description: Cryptographic random values generated for session tracking — phishing kit victim fingerprinting
sourcePatterns.note: Generating cryptographic random values for session/tracking IDs with names like victimId or trackId is a phishing kit fingerprinting technique for tracking individual victims across the multi-step flow.

sourcePatterns.description: File download with double extension — malware disguised as document
sourcePatterns.note: Files with double extensions like document.pdf.exe are malware disguised as documents. The first extension appears legitimate while the actual executable extension is the one Windows uses to run the file.

sourcePatterns.description: html2canvas or similar library used to screenshot the page — captures filled credentials visually
sourcePatterns.note: Using html2canvas to screenshot the page after the victim fills in credentials, then sending the screenshot via fetch/XHR/Telegram, is a phishing kit technique that captures credentials visually — it works even if the password field is obfuscated.

sourcePatterns.description: WalletConnect-style wallet connection popup dynamically injected — crypto drainer technique
sourcePatterns.note: Wallet connection popups that immediately request eth_requestAccounts or signing operations are characteristic of crypto drainer scripts. The connection is used to initiate malicious token approvals.

sourcePatterns.description: eth_signTypedData_v4 request for token approval — crypto drainer permit signature
sourcePatterns.note: Requesting eth_signTypedData with permit/approve/setApprovalForAll parameters is the signature crypto drainer technique. The victim signs a message that grants the attacker unlimited token transfer approval.

sourcePatterns.description: Inferno Drainer configuration endpoint with access key — drainer-as-a-service signature
sourcePatterns.note: Inferno Drainer and similar DaaS kits communicate with C2 APIs using access keys. The nftfastapi.com, seaport-api, and claimapi domains with config/drain endpoints are definitive drainer signatures.

sourcePatterns.description: X-Evilginx header artifact in page source — Evilginx AiTM proxy signature
sourcePatterns.note: The X-Evilginx header is an easter egg in unmodified Evilginx installations that leaks the proxy's presence. Many operators fail to remove this header. Definitive AiTM proxy indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Evilginx-style JavaScript injection with obfuscator.io pattern in proxied page
sourcePatterns.note: Evilginx Pro uses obfuscator.io to obfuscate injected JavaScript. The combination of _0x hex variables with phishing-related keywords (phishlet, lure, redirect) in the same script block indicates Evilginx js_inject.

sourcePatterns.description: Progressive Web App install prompt on login page — PWA phishing technique
sourcePatterns.note: Triggering a PWA install prompt on a login page creates a persistent phishing app on the victim's device. The installed PWA looks like a legitimate app icon but opens the phishing page without browser URL bar.

sourcePatterns.description: Web app manifest referencing login/sign-in — PWA phishing app configuration
sourcePatterns.note: A web app manifest on a page with login elements indicates a PWA phishing app. When installed, the app icon on the home screen opens the phishing page in a standalone window without browser URL bar.

sourcePatterns.description: ClearFake-style iframe overlay replacing page content with fake update lure
sourcePatterns.note: ClearFake creates a full-page fixed iframe overlay that hides the real website content and shows the fake update lure. The iframe has high z-index and 100% width to cover the entire page.

sourcePatterns.description: EtherHiding technique — payload retrieved from Binance Smart Chain smart contract
sourcePatterns.note: EtherHiding retrieves malicious payloads from Binance Smart Chain smart contracts. This makes the payload immutable and impossible to take down since it's stored on the blockchain. Definitive advanced malware delivery technique.

sourcePatterns.description: Astronomy or Bible-related code comments — Cephas phishing kit signature
sourcePatterns.note: The Cephas phishing kit contains distinctive astronomy and Bible-related code comments like 'wine tasting on Riverside Avenue' and 'Spectral Quasar'. These thematic anomalies serve as kit fingerprints and content diversification.

sourcePatterns.description: CoGUI kit signature — CAPTCHA gate with brand impersonation targeting specific verticals
sourcePatterns.note: CoGUI phishing kit uses CAPTCHA gates combined with specific brand targeting (Rakuten, Amazon, Apple, PayPal). After CAPTCHA verification, victims are redirected to the brand-specific phishing page.

sourcePatterns.description: NDSW/Parrot TDS JavaScript injection marker — traffic distribution system for malware delivery
sourcePatterns.note: The NDSW/NDSJ/NDSX variable assignment is the signature of Parrot TDS (Traffic Distribution System) injections. Compromised websites containing these markers redirect visitors to SocGholish fake update pages. Definitive malware indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Keitaro TDS redirect pattern — traffic distribution system used by TA569/SocGholish
sourcePatterns.note: Keitaro TDS parameters (click_id, campaign_id, landing_id) in JavaScript redirects indicate a traffic distribution system routing victims to phishing or malware pages. TA569 uses Keitaro extensively.

sourcePatterns.description: WordPress plugin path with suspicious script injection — compromised site indicator
sourcePatterns.note: Suspicious asynchronous script loads from WordPress plugin directories (pattern: /wp-content/plugins/plugin-name/xyz-script.js) indicate fake plugins injected via stolen credentials for ClearFake/ClickFix delivery.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram bot exfiltration with formatted message — structured credential report to attacker
sourcePatterns.note: Telegram bot sendMessage calls with formatted text containing credential field labels (email, user, pass, ip, country) using newlines or HTML formatting is the structured exfiltration report format used by phishing kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook with formatted embed — structured credential exfiltration report
sourcePatterns.note: Discord webhook calls with embed objects containing credential field labels are structured exfiltration reports. The attacker receives a nicely formatted Discord message with the victim's stolen credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Mamba 2FA signature — Socket.IO library with template script and sti/vic HTML attributes
sourcePatterns.note: Mamba 2FA uses Socket.IO for real-time WebSocket communication with relay servers. The sti (customer ID, double base64) and vic (victim email) HTML attributes are definitive Mamba 2FA signatures. 10M+ attacks in late 2025.

sourcePatterns.description: Mamba 2FA template script filenames — jsnom, jsdrive, jssp, jsv templates
sourcePatterns.note: Mamba 2FA uses specific template script filenames: jsnom.js (Microsoft sign-in), jsdrive.js (OneDrive), jssp.js (SharePoint), jsv.js (voice mail). These filenames are definitive kit signatures.

sourcePatterns.description: Mamba 2FA Socket.IO event names — new-session, password_command, otp_command relay events
sourcePatterns.note: Mamba 2FA Socket.IO events: new-session (init), password_command (relay password), otp_command (relay MFA code), s2c_cookies (receive stolen cookies), s2c_restart (restart flow). These event names are definitive signatures.

sourcePatterns.description: Sneaky 2FA food-themed decoy page — 'Gourmet Delights and Beverage' initial page content
sourcePatterns.note: Sneaky 2FA initially loads a page titled 'Gourmet Delights and Beverage' with food-related content before reloading to show the Cloudflare Turnstile challenge. This exact title string is a definitive Sneaky 2FA signature.

sourcePatterns.description: Sneaky 2FA title pattern — randomized verify/confirm title from known list
sourcePatterns.note: Sneaky 2FA randomly selects page titles from a specific list: 'Verify your account/identity/credentials/informations/email/login', 'Confirm your ...', 'Signin to your account'. The exact set of alternatives is a Sneaky 2FA signature.

sourcePatterns.description: Sneaky 2FA blurred Microsoft login background — base64-encoded transparent brand background
sourcePatterns.note: Sneaky 2FA uses a blurred screenshot of a legitimate Microsoft login page as background, creating the illusion that the victim is signing into a real Microsoft service. The blur+Microsoft branding combination is characteristic.

sourcePatterns.description: Supabase API endpoint used for credential exfiltration — serverless database abuse
sourcePatterns.note: Supabase provides free PostgreSQL databases with REST APIs. Phishing kits use Supabase as a serverless credential store, writing stolen data directly to the database without any server-side code.

sourcePatterns.description: Firebase Realtime Database used for credential exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Firebase Realtime Database (.firebaseio.com) is abused for credential exfiltration. Phishing kits write stolen credentials directly to Firebase using simple REST PUT/POST requests.

sourcePatterns.description: Airtable API used as credential exfiltration endpoint — spreadsheet database abuse
sourcePatterns.note: Airtable's API (api.airtable.com/v0/appXXXX) is abused by phishing kits to write credentials to Airtable bases. The appXXX ID is unique to each attacker's base.

sourcePatterns.description: Notion API used for credential exfiltration — database abuse
sourcePatterns.note: Notion's API is abused by phishing kits to write stolen credentials to Notion databases. The structured API (pages, databases, blocks) provides a free, organized exfiltration backend.

sourcePatterns.description: N0123N separator used between base64 parameter and email address — Mamba 2FA URL marker
sourcePatterns.note: The string N0123N is used by Mamba 2FA as a separator between the base64-encoded parameters and the victim's email address in phishing URLs. This exact string is a definitive Mamba 2FA signature with zero false positive risk.

sourcePatterns.description: WebRTC DataChannel used for credential/payment data exfiltration — CSP-bypassing technique
sourcePatterns.note: WebRTC DataChannel exfiltration bypasses CSP entirely because CSP doesn't restrict WebRTC traffic. Discovered in March 2026 Magecart attack against automotive e-commerce. The DataChannel sends stolen data over DTLS-encrypted UDP, invisible to HTTP network inspection.

sourcePatterns.description: WebRTC STUN/TURN server configured for data exfiltration via username field
sourcePatterns.note: Exfiltrating data via the username field of STUN/TURN server configuration. The credential data is sent as part of the ICE negotiation handshake, bypassing all HTTP-level security controls.

sourcePatterns.description: CSP report-uri used as data exfiltration channel — abuses CSP violation reporting
sourcePatterns.note: Abusing CSP report-uri to exfiltrate data by intentionally triggering CSP violations. The violation report includes the blocked-uri containing stolen data, which is sent to the attacker's report collector. CSP reports bypass same-origin restrictions.

sourcePatterns.description: Web Share API used to exfiltrate data by triggering native share dialog
sourcePatterns.note: Abusing navigator.share() to trigger the native share dialog with credential data pre-filled is a novel exfiltration technique. If the victim shares via email or messaging, the stolen data is sent through a legitimate channel.

sourcePatterns.description: File API used to construct downloadable credential file — offline data harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Constructing a downloadable file containing stolen credentials via the File API is an offline exfiltration technique. The victim unknowingly downloads a file containing their own credentials, which malware on their device can later exfiltrate.

sourcePatterns.description: Pop-under window opened behind the current window — stealthy phishing window
sourcePatterns.note: Opening a window and immediately blurring it or moving it offscreen creates a pop-under phishing page. The user doesn't notice it, but when they close their main window, the phishing page is revealed.

sourcePatterns.description: PWA manifest with standalone display mode on credential page — app-like phishing install
sourcePatterns.note: A PWA manifest with display:standalone or display:fullscreen on a page with login elements creates an app-like experience without a visible URL bar. When added to the home screen, the phishing page looks identical to a native app.

sourcePatterns.description: WebAPK install triggered through Chrome's PWA installation mechanism — silent APK generation
sourcePatterns.note: Chrome on Android can silently generate a WebAPK from a PWA, creating a native-looking app without going through the Play Store. Phishing kits abuse this to install persistent phishing 'apps' that bypass store security review.

sourcePatterns.description: Apple mobile web app capable meta tag on login page — iOS PWA phishing configuration
sourcePatterns.note: Setting apple-mobile-web-app-capable to yes on a login page enables iOS standalone mode. When saved to the home screen, the phishing page runs without Safari's address bar, hiding the phishing domain.

sourcePatterns.description: Android intent:// URI scheme used for deep linking to app or malicious activity
sourcePatterns.note: Android intent:// URIs with #Intent parameters can launch apps, open URLs, or trigger actions. Phishing pages abuse intent:// schemes with browser_fallback_url to redirect to phishing pages when the target app isn't installed.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS targeting standalone display mode to hide URL indicators — PWA phishing technique
sourcePatterns.note: CSS media queries targeting display-mode:standalone to hide navigation elements are used by PWA phishing pages. When installed as a standalone PWA, the page hides any visible URL indicators to prevent the user from checking the domain.

sourcePatterns.description: Clipboard monitoring for cryptocurrency address replacement — mobile crypto theft
sourcePatterns.note: Monitoring the clipboard for cryptocurrency wallet addresses (Ethereum 0x..., Bitcoin bc1/1.../3..., Tron T...) and replacing them with the attacker's address is a mobile crypto clipboard hijacker. Victims unknowingly send funds to the attacker.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram Bot API sendDocument endpoint — file upload exfiltration of credential archives
sourcePatterns.note: The sendDocument API endpoint uploads files up to 50MB to a Telegram chat. Phishing kits and infostealers use it to exfiltrate credential archives, screenshots, and cookie databases. 3.8% of all malware campaigns in 2024-2025 used Telegram sendDocument for exfil.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram Bot API sendPhoto endpoint — screenshot exfiltration to attacker chat
sourcePatterns.note: The sendPhoto API endpoint sends images to a Telegram chat. Phishing kits use it to exfiltrate screenshots of the victim's filled credential form (via html2canvas) or the victim's desktop.

sourcePatterns.description: Base64-encoded Telegram bot token decoded at runtime — obfuscated exfil endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing kits base64-encode the Telegram bot token and API URL to evade static pattern matching. The atob() call decoding a string followed by Telegram-related keywords indicates obfuscated Telegram exfiltration.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram API URL constructed via string concatenation — evasion of URL pattern matching
sourcePatterns.note: Constructing the Telegram API URL via string concatenation ('api' + '.' + 'telegram' + '.org') breaks static pattern matching for 'api.telegram.org'. This is the most common evasion technique for Telegram-based exfiltration.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram chat_id parameter with numeric group/channel ID — identifies exfiltration destination
sourcePatterns.note: The chat_id parameter identifies the Telegram chat receiving exfiltrated data. Private chats use positive IDs (6-10 digits), groups use negative IDs (starting with -), and supergroups/channels use -100 prefix. Finding chat_id with a numeric value in JS confirms Telegram exfiltration.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram bot token format in JavaScript — numeric:alphanumeric authentication credential
sourcePatterns.note: Telegram bot tokens follow the format NNNNNNNNN:XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX (8-10 digit bot ID : 35 char alphanumeric secret). Finding this pattern in a variable named bot/token/tg/telegram is a definitive exfil credential.

sourcePatterns.description: URL-encoded Telegram API endpoint — obfuscation via percent-encoding
sourcePatterns.note: URL-encoding the Telegram API string ('api' = %61%70%69, 'telegram' = %74%65%6c..., 'sendMessage' = %73%65%6e%64...) evades string matching. Percent-encoded Telegram keywords in JavaScript are definitive obfuscated exfil indicators.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram channel or profile page fetched for C2 configuration — payload URL extraction
sourcePatterns.note: Fetching a Telegram channel or profile page to extract embedded C2 URLs or configuration data is a technique used by Lumma Stealer and similar malware. The bot's profile bio contains the next-stage payload URL.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram exfiltration message with structured IP/geo/browser metadata — phishkit report format
sourcePatterns.note: Structured messages containing IP/UserAgent/Country/City/Browser metadata sent to Telegram are phishkit victim reports. The attacker receives a formatted intelligence brief on each victim alongside their credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook URL with complete token — credential exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Full Discord webhook URLs with numeric ID (17-20 digits) and token (60+ chars) are definitive exfiltration endpoints. The webhook allows sending messages and files to any Discord channel without authentication.

sourcePatterns.description: Legacy discordapp.com webhook URL — older phishing kits still use the deprecated domain
sourcePatterns.note: The legacy discordapp.com domain still works for webhook API calls. Older phishing kits that haven't been updated still use this domain. Both discord.com and discordapp.com webhooks should be detected.

sourcePatterns.description: Base64-encoded Discord webhook URL decoded at runtime — obfuscated exfil endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing kits base64-encode Discord webhook URLs to evade static detection. The atob() decode followed by discord/webhook keywords indicates obfuscated Discord exfiltration.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook URL constructed via string concatenation — evasion technique
sourcePatterns.note: Constructing the Discord webhook URL via string concatenation ('discord' + '.com' + '/api/' + 'webhooks/') breaks pattern matching. Same evasion technique as Telegram string concatenation.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord bot token in Authorization header — bot-based credential exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Discord bot tokens in Authorization headers follow the format XXX.XXXXXX.XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX. A bot token in page JavaScript enables sending messages to any channel the bot has access to, bypassing webhook-only restrictions.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook with custom avatar/username — branded exfiltration report
sourcePatterns.note: Discord webhooks with custom avatar_url and username set to phishing-related names (e.g., 'Credential Logger', 'Victim Alert') indicate branded exfiltration bots. The attacker customizes the webhook appearance for their campaign.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram Bot API forwardMessage — relaying messages between C2 chats
sourcePatterns.note: The forwardMessage API endpoint relays messages between Telegram chats. Phishing kits use this to forward captured credentials from a collection bot to multiple operator channels, enabling credential distribution across a criminal team.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram Bot API getUpdates — polling for C2 commands from attacker
sourcePatterns.note: The getUpdates API endpoint retrieves messages sent to the bot, used as a C2 polling mechanism. The phishing page checks for commands from the attacker (e.g., 'show MFA prompt', 'redirect to brand site') via bot messages.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram Bot API sendMessage with inline_keyboard — interactive phishing control panel
sourcePatterns.note: Sending messages with inline_keyboard buttons creates an interactive control panel for the attacker in Telegram. Buttons can approve/reject victims, trigger MFA prompts, or redirect the victim — all controlled via Telegram.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram sendMessage with HTML parse_mode — formatted credential exfiltration report
sourcePatterns.note: Setting parse_mode to HTML enables rich formatting in Telegram messages. Phishing kits use HTML tags (<b>, <code>, <pre>) to create structured, readable credential reports with bold labels and monospace values.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple exfiltration channels in same page — Telegram AND Discord or email
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing kits using multiple exfiltration channels (Telegram + Discord, Telegram + email) provide redundancy — if one channel is taken down, credentials still flow through the other. Finding both Telegram and Discord references in the same script is a strong phishing indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Arabic Facebook security alert phishing lure
Full wallet interface presented as single clickable image
Entire page wrapped in single external redirect link
Malicious MSI installer distribution with OS restriction
WhatsApp security center phishing in Chinese
Fake Apple transaction alert urging user to call a phone number — tech support scam pattern
Modal with pulse animation and call-now button pattern used in Apple tech support scams
Detects fake transaction alert pages that urge users to call a phone number to prevent unauthorized activity
Detects fear/urgency keywords in the page hostname pattern commonly used in phishing domains (security-unauthorised, unauthorized-access, etc.)
Detects domains using security-urgency keywords (unauthorised, alert, verification, suspended) combined with brand names in subdomain
Detects Meta ad account deletion phishing template with appeal lure
Social engineering text claiming email login required for protected file access as rightful recipient
sourcePatterns.patternString: (?:required to login|rightful recipient)[\s\S]{0,300}(?:protected file|secure document|unauthorized access is prohibited)

sourcePatterns.description: Fake loading animation with timed redirect — creates illusion of legitimate processing after credential capture
sourcePatterns.note: A timed loading/spinner animation followed by a redirect is the classic phishkit 'we're verifying your credentials' trick. It buys time for exfiltration while the victim sees a fake progress indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Session expired or account suspended urgency messaging in page source
sourcePatterns.note: Urgency messaging combining account status warnings with login prompts is a core social engineering technique. The chain of suspended/expired + verify/login is highly specific to phishing pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Action required or verify identity urgency text combined with form elements
sourcePatterns.note: Urgency text like 'action required' or 'verify your identity' directly followed by a form element within 500 chars is characteristic of phishing. Low weight because some legitimate 2FA flows use similar language.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake incorrect password error message prompting re-entry — multi-capture technique
sourcePatterns.note: Showing a fake 'incorrect password' error and clearing the password field to capture a second credential attempt is a phishing technique that doubles the chance of getting the real password. The kit always shows an error on first attempt.

sourcePatterns.description: SweetAlert library used for fake security notification popups on phishing pages
sourcePatterns.note: SweetAlert is a legitimate popup library, but phishing kits use it heavily for fake 'your account has been compromised' alert modals. Low weight because many real sites use it, but it stacks as a supporting signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Countdown timer creating urgency on a page with form inputs — social engineering pressure
sourcePatterns.note: Countdown timers on credential capture pages create artificial urgency ('Your session expires in 5:00'). This social engineering technique pressures victims into entering credentials quickly without scrutinizing the page.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake error message combined with brand login prompt — error-based social engineering
sourcePatterns.note: Fake error messages paired with login prompts and brand names create urgency ('There was a problem with your Microsoft account. Please sign in to verify.'). This social engineering pattern is used across many phishing campaigns.

sourcePatterns.description: Shared document or file notification lure text in page source
sourcePatterns.note: Shared document lure text ('Someone has shared a document with you. Sign in to view.') is the #1 phishing email-to-page conversion technique for business email compromise. This text on a login page is a strong phishing indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Voicemail notification lure text combined with login prompt — voicemail phishing campaign
sourcePatterns.note: Voicemail notification lures ('You have a new voicemail. Sign in to listen.') are a prevalent phishing technique targeting business users. The combination of voicemail text with login prompts is highly specific to phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: French-language banking urgency text in phishing page — targets French-speaking users
sourcePatterns.note: French-language account suspension and verification urgency text ('votre compte a été bloqué', 'veuillez confirmer votre identité') targets French-speaking victims. This social engineering text combined with a login form is a strong phishing indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Portuguese-language banking urgency text in phishing page — targets Brazilian/Portuguese users
sourcePatterns.note: Portuguese-language account urgency text ('sua conta foi bloqueada', 'confirme sua identidade', 'atualize seus dados') targets Brazilian and Portuguese victims.

sourcePatterns.description: German-language banking urgency text — targets German-speaking users
sourcePatterns.note: German-language account urgency text ('Ihr Konto wurde gesperrt', 'bestätigen Sie Ihre Identität') targets German-speaking banking phishing victims.

sourcePatterns.description: Japanese-language urgency text in phishing page — targets Japanese users
sourcePatterns.patternString: (?:アカウント[がは](?:停止|制限|ロック|無効)|(?:本人)?確認[がをの](?:必要|お願い)|情報[をの](?:更新|確認)(?:してください|が必要))
sourcePatterns.note: Japanese-language account urgency text ('アカウントが停止', '本人確認が必要', '情報を更新してください') targets Japanese users with account suspension and verification lures.

sourcePatterns.description: Korean-language urgency text in phishing page — targets Korean users
sourcePatterns.note: Korean-language account urgency text ('계정이 정지', '본인 확인을 해주세요') targets Korean users with account suspension and identity verification phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Toll road payment form with small dollar amount — smishing toll scam indicator
sourcePatterns.note: Toll road payment pages showing small dollar amounts ($3-$12 typically) combined with toll brand names are the hallmark of the massive 2025-2026 Chinese smishing campaigns. The small amount makes victims less suspicious.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake delivery redelivery fee or shipping fee payment form — logistics smishing scam
sourcePatterns.note: Fake redelivery fees or customs charges are the hook for logistics phishing. USPS, DHL, FedEx, and UPS impersonation pages request small payment amounts to harvest credit card details.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake subscription renewal or expiration warning — McAfee/Norton/antivirus phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Fake subscription renewal scams impersonating McAfee, Norton, and other software are a top phishing vector. They create urgency about 'expired protection' to harvest payment information.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake cryptocurrency airdrop claim page with wallet connection prompt
sourcePatterns.note: Fake airdrop/free mint/whitelist claim pages that prompt wallet connection are the primary crypto drainer delivery mechanism. The 'claim + connect wallet + wallet brand' triple is definitive.

sourcePatterns.description: Push notification permission requested with deceptive prompts — notification spam/phishing vector
sourcePatterns.note: Requesting push notification permission with deceptive prompts ('allow to verify you're human') tricks users into subscribing to malicious push notifications that later deliver phishing links.

sourcePatterns.description: Tel: link with fake support number — voice phishing (vishing) lure on phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: Clickable phone number links labeled as support/help/customer service on phishing pages are vishing lures. The fake support number is answered by scammers who complete the credential theft over the phone.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake live chat widget on phishing page — social engineering through fake support interaction
sourcePatterns.note: Fake live chat widgets on phishing pages create the illusion of a legitimate support channel. Scammers may guide victims through entering credentials or personal information via the fake chat.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake browser update notification with download prompt — SocGholish/ClearFake/FakeBat delivery
sourcePatterns.note: Fake browser update notifications are the primary delivery mechanism for SocGholish, ClearFake, FakeBat, and RogueRaticate malware. The 'browser out of date + download' combination on any website that isn't an official browser vendor is a definitive malware lure.

sourcePatterns.description: Tech support scam page with fake error/BSOD and phone number — browser lockscreen attack
sourcePatterns.note: Tech support scam pages showing fake virus/trojan/computer locked messages with a phone number to call. These pages often use requestFullscreen() to simulate a locked computer.

sourcePatterns.description: requestFullscreen used to simulate locked computer — tech support scam technique
sourcePatterns.note: Using requestFullscreen() to make a fake error page take over the entire screen simulates a locked/crashed computer. Combined with fake error text and a phone number, this is a definitive tech support scam.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript file download disguised as browser update — SocGholish payload delivery
sourcePatterns.note: Download links or prompts for files named 'Update.js', 'Update.zip', or similar are the SocGholish/ClearFake payload delivery mechanism. The fake update downloads a malicious JavaScript file.

sourcePatterns.description: Invoice payment or wire transfer urgency text — BEC landing page indicator
sourcePatterns.note: Invoice/payment urgency text combined with payment action prompts is characteristic of Business Email Compromise landing pages. These harvest banking details or redirect victims to payment fraud pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Geek Squad / Best Buy subscription renewal scam — tech support phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Geek Squad/Best Buy fake renewal scams claim a subscription has auto-renewed for a large amount ($249.99-$499.99) and provide a phone number to call for cancellation. Top tech support scam vector.

sourcePatterns.description: Audio alarm or alert sound played on scam page — tech support scare tactic
sourcePatterns.note: Playing alarm/siren sounds on loop is a tech support scam scare tactic. The audio creates panic and urgency, pressuring victims to call the fake support number displayed on the page.

sourcePatterns.description: Browser speech synthesis used to voice fake warnings — audio social engineering
sourcePatterns.note: Using the Web Speech API to read aloud fake security warnings ('Your computer is infected') adds an audio dimension to tech support scams. The voice message creates urgency and panic.

sourcePatterns.description: Fullscreen mode with fake browser chrome and URL bar — URL spoofing attack
sourcePatterns.note: Going fullscreen and rendering a fake browser chrome with a fake URL bar showing a legitimate brand domain is a sophisticated URL spoofing technique. The user sees what appears to be a real browser address bar but it's an HTML rendering.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake biometric authentication prompt — fingerprint/face ID phishing UI
sourcePatterns.note: Fake biometric authentication prompts that actually fall back to requesting a password/PIN are a social engineering technique. The 'biometric failed, enter your password' flow tricks users into thinking they're using secure authentication.

sourcePatterns.description: Notification API permission granted with phishing notification content
sourcePatterns.note: Browser notifications containing urgency text (expired, locked, security) with click handlers redirect victims to phishing pages. The notification appears to come from the browser itself, increasing trust.

sourcePatterns.description: Android APK download prompted on phishing page — mobile malware delivery
sourcePatterns.note: APK file downloads prompted from phishing pages are mobile malware delivery. The fake page impersonates a legitimate app and tricks users into sideloading a malicious Android application.

sourcePatterns.description: MSIX/APPX Windows app package download — fake update malware delivery
sourcePatterns.note: MSIX/APPX Windows app package downloads from phishing pages deliver malware disguised as legitimate Windows applications. This is a SocGholish/ClearFake delivery variant.

sourcePatterns.description: Spanish-language banking urgency text — targets Spanish-speaking users
sourcePatterns.note: Spanish-language account urgency text ('su cuenta ha sido bloqueada', 'verifique su identidad') targets Spanish-speaking users in Latin America and Spain.

sourcePatterns.description: Chinese-language urgency text in phishing page — targets Chinese-speaking users
sourcePatterns.patternString: (?:您的账[号户][已被](?:冻结|锁定|限制|暂停)|请(?:验证|确认)(?:您的|your)(?:身份|账户|信息)|立即(?:更新|验证|确认))
sourcePatterns.note: Chinese-language account urgency text ('您的账号已被冻结', '请验证您的身份', '立即更新') targets Chinese-speaking users with account suspension and verification phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake Google Play Store page impersonating app listing — malicious APK/PWA delivery
sourcePatterns.note: Fake Google Play Store pages showing app listings with install buttons, ratings, and download counts are used to deliver malicious APKs or PWAs. The page impersonates Google Play's UI to build trust for sideloading malware.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake Apple App Store page impersonating app listing — malicious iOS profile/PWA delivery
sourcePatterns.note: Fake Apple App Store pages with app listings, ratings, and GET/Download buttons trick iOS users into installing malicious profiles, PWAs, or enterprise-signed apps outside the real App Store.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake iOS Smart App Banner prompting app install — impersonates native iOS UI element
sourcePatterns.note: Fake Smart App Banners using apple-itunes-app meta tags trick iOS users into thinking there's a native app to install. The banner links to a phishing PWA or sideloaded enterprise app instead of the real App Store.

sourcePatterns.description: SMS or tel: scheme URI used in page links — triggers phone call or text from browser
sourcePatterns.note: SMS URIs with pre-filled body text (sms:+1234?body=...) can auto-compose messages on the victim's phone. Phishing pages use these to make victims send attacker-controlled text to premium numbers or other victims.

sourcePatterns.description: WhatsApp share link with pre-filled phishing message — social engineering amplification
sourcePatterns.note: WhatsApp share links (wa.me/send?text=...) with pre-filled URLs let phishing pages amplify attacks by making victims share phishing links with their contacts. The shared link comes from a trusted contact.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram share link with pre-filled phishing URL — social engineering amplification
sourcePatterns.note: Telegram share links (t.me/share/url?url=...) with pre-filled phishing URLs amplify campaigns by making victims share malicious links within their Telegram contacts.

sourcePatterns.description: Mobile carrier billing or account verification form — carrier impersonation phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Mobile carrier billing/account verification pages that request payment updates are used in carrier impersonation phishing targeting T-Mobile, Verizon, AT&T, and other providers.

sourcePatterns.description: Custom 'Add to Home Screen' instruction overlay for PWA install — mobile app phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Custom overlay instructions telling users how to 'Add to Home Screen' guide victims through installing a phishing PWA as an app. The instructions are tailored to iOS (share button → Add to Home Screen) or Android (menu → Install App).

sourcePatterns.description: Mobile status bar elements rendered in HTML — spoofing native device UI
sourcePatterns.note: Rendering a fake mobile status bar (signal bars, battery, time, 4G/5G) in HTML positioned at the top of the page makes the phishing page look like it's running inside a native app rather than a browser.

sourcePatterns.description: Haptic/vibration feedback simulating authentication confirmation — fake success feeling
sourcePatterns.note: Using the Vibration API to provide haptic feedback when credentials are 'verified' creates a convincing physical confirmation feeling on the victim's phone. The vibration makes the fake authentication feel real.

sourcePatterns.description: All-numeric domain pattern often used for phishing
Western brand impersonation on Chinese domains
Random subdomain on GoDaddy website builder indicating potential phishing
Discord official page title on non-Discord domain
sourcePatterns.patternString: <title[^>]*>Discord \| Your Place to Talk and Hang Out</title>

sourcePatterns.description: Detects page titles claiming to be 'Official Apple' support/contact pages on non-Apple domains
Detects Unicode homoglyph characters (mathematical script, accented Latin) used in title or meta tags to spell crypto/financial brand names while evading text matching — e.g. G𝓮miñi for Gemini
Detects Microsoft account sign-in page title impersonation used by credential harvesting phishkits
Detects Cloudflare challenge impersonation pages with common misspelling 'Challange' in title or body
Detects pages using Dropbox sign-in page title patterns with credential fields, indicating brand impersonation when served from non-Dropbox domains
Detects DocuSign login page title impersonation pattern in HTML title or visible text
Page title contains Instagram login/signin branding on a non-Instagram domain
Page title containing 'Log in' or 'Log into' with 'Facebook' on non-Facebook domain
Page title containing 'Outlook 365 Login' — used by phishing kits impersonating Microsoft Outlook; real Microsoft pages never use this exact title
Generic Amazon project title commonly used on Amazon clone/impersonation sites
sourcePatterns.note: Amateur Amazon impersonation sites often use generic titles like 'Amazon Project' instead of legitimate Amazon page titles

sourcePatterns.description: Dynamic document.title manipulation to impersonate a major brand login page
sourcePatterns.note: Setting document.title dynamically to include both a login prompt and a major brand name is a phishing indicator. Legitimate login pages set their title server-side.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating Zoom meeting or sign-in page
sourcePatterns.note: Zoom meeting join and login page title impersonation on non-Zoom domains. Zoom phishing is prevalent for business credential theft.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating WeTransfer file download page
sourcePatterns.note: WeTransfer title impersonation is used in file-sharing phishing campaigns that trick users into entering credentials to 'download' shared files.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating DHL tracking or delivery page
sourcePatterns.note: DHL tracking page impersonation is a top logistics phishing vector. Fake delivery notification pages harvest personal and payment information.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating USPS tracking or redelivery page
sourcePatterns.note: USPS impersonation is one of the most common logistics phishing campaigns, especially targeting US users with fake redelivery fee pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating FedEx tracking or delivery page
sourcePatterns.note: FedEx tracking page impersonation targets users with fake delivery notifications that harvest personal or payment information.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating LinkedIn login or notification page
sourcePatterns.note: LinkedIn login impersonation targets business professionals. Credential theft from LinkedIn accounts enables BEC and social engineering attacks.

iTrustCapital typosquat - missing 'i' in 'capital'

Detects hostnames containing 'bmo' combined with banking-related keywords (online, banking, login, secure, business) on non-BMO domains

Increase weight for deep subdomains on Indonesian business domains

IPFS-hosted pages with password fields indicating credential harvesting
Suspicious use of .bond TLD with logistics-related subdomains

Detects 'my' prefix hostnames on high-risk TLDs (.icu, .top, .xyz, .sbs) commonly used for banking portal phishing
Detects random alphanumeric prefix followed by brand-related keyword in subdomain, indicating wildcard DNS abuse on compromised domain
URL query parameter selects brand name for phishkit template rendering (e.g. ?d=swedbank, ?brand=paypal)
Base64-encoded Microsoft OAuth URL found in query parameter — characteristic of phishkits that proxy Microsoft login flows through compromised domains
Detects domains using brand-job/career/hiring pattern with fake meeting or reference IDs commonly used in employment scam phishkits
Constant Contact landing page URL pattern commonly abused for phishing
Detects Google Sites pages where the URL path contains a third-party domain (attacker's Workspace domain) — indicates abuse of Google Sites for brand impersonation
Detects base64-encoded email addresses in URL query parameters, common in targeted phishing campaigns
URL path contains a base64-encoded string that likely encodes an email address (contains @ when decoded), indicating per-victim phishing targeting
Detects domains combining login/service keywords on .cloud TLD with randomized short path identifiers typical of phishing campaign infrastructure

Page on duckdns.org free DDNS contains an HTML form — extremely high phishing correlation
Venmo-branded PHP logging endpoint in URL structure
sourcePatterns.note: Detects Venmo-specific credential harvesting PHP endpoints in HTML attributes

sourcePatterns.description: Venmo-branded URL with PHP credential harvesting endpoint and randomized session path
sourcePatterns.note: Specific to Venmo phishkits using PHP backends with characteristic naming conventions - legitimate Venmo never uses PHP endpoints

sourcePatterns.description: Base64-encoded email address detected in URL parameter within page links
sourcePatterns.note: Base64-encoded values in URL parameters named email/user/login/id/token indicate personalized phishing where the victim's email is encoded in the URL for tracking or form pre-fill.

sourcePatterns.description: Form action URL pointing to a suspicious TLD domain — exfiltration to cheap/free domains
sourcePatterns.note: Form actions pointing to domains on suspicious TLDs (.top, .xyz, .buzz, .live, .icu, .tk, etc.) indicate credential exfiltration to cheap/free domain infrastructure. These TLDs are disproportionately used for phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript redirect to suspicious TLD — phishing redirect chain endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript redirects to suspicious TLDs indicate the page is part of a phishing redirect chain that bounces through multiple domains.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple sequential redirects in JavaScript — phishing redirect chain
sourcePatterns.note: Two or more redirect assignments in the same script indicate a redirect chain. Phishing kits bounce through multiple intermediary pages before landing on the final credential harvesting page.

sourcePatterns.description: URL shortener used in JavaScript redirect — obscured phishing destination
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting through URL shorteners in JavaScript hides the final phishing destination from both users and URL-based detection. No legitimate login page redirects through shortener services.

sourcePatterns.description: DuckDNS dynamic DNS domain in page links — free DNS commonly abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: DuckDNS is a free dynamic DNS service heavily abused for phishing infrastructure. The .duckdns.org subdomain indicates attacker-controlled dynamic DNS infrastructure.

sourcePatterns.description: Google open redirect URLs used in page source — abuses Google's trusted domain for redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Using Google's open redirect endpoints (google.com/url?q=, google.com/amp/s/) to bounce through Google's domain makes phishing links appear to come from Google. This abuses Google's trusted reputation.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft open redirect URLs used in page source — abuses Microsoft's trusted domain
sourcePatterns.note: Abusing Microsoft's open redirectors to bounce phishing links through Microsoft's trusted domain. Common in BEC campaigns targeting Office 365 users.

sourcePatterns.description: Custom app deep link schemes in page source — potential app-based phishing redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Custom URI schemes for messaging apps (whatsapp://, telegram://, signal://) in page links can deep-link into apps with pre-filled malicious content, contact additions, or group invites used in social engineering chains.

sourcePatterns.description: Chinese .TOP domain in page source links — Smishing Triad infrastructure indicator
sourcePatterns.note: The Smishing Triad predominantly uses .TOP domains for their toll road, delivery, and payment phishing campaigns. Links to .top domains combined with logistics/toll/payment keywords are characteristic of this Chinese smishing group.

sourcePatterns.description: Link-in-bio service used as phishing redirect — abuses social media link tools
sourcePatterns.note: Link-in-bio services (Linktree, Beacons, Stan, Bio.link) are abused as phishing redirectors because their domains are trusted and rarely blocked. The phishing link hides behind a legitimate social media link tool.
